title: Bathroom Parameter Manifest
description: Six knobs an LLM front-end may turn for bathroom.

ceiling_height_m.description: Floor-to-ceiling clear height of the bathroom.
ceiling_height_m.rationale: Ceiling height modulates perceived enclosure and approach-avoidance decisions. Bathrooms with lower ceilings (1.8-2.1m) produce stronger enclosure effects than any other room type due to their small footprint.

daylight_intensity.description: Scalar on the natural light contribution through windows or skylights.
daylight_intensity.rationale: Daylight access in bathrooms modulates morning circadian entrainment and perceived cleanliness. Münch et al. (2020) identify bathrooms as a critical site for morning light exposure.

tile_material_id.description: Named material token for wall and floor tile surface.
tile_material_id.rationale: Surface material warmth and cleanliness cues contribute directly to stress reduction and perceived hygiene. Ulrich (1991) identifies material texture as a primary wellness variable in healthcare interiors.

fixture_count.description: Number of fixture entities placed: toilet, sink, shower, bathtub, towel rail, mirror.
fixture_count.rationale: Fixture density controls perceived clutter and cognitive load. Joye (2007) links object density to complexity perception and restorative capacity.

wall_warmth_index.description: 0 = cool palette (blues, greys); 1 = warm palette (creams, terracottas).
wall_warmth_index.rationale: Colour temperature of bathroom surfaces affects perceived warmth and stress recovery. Ulrich (1991) reports warm material palettes reduce physiological stress markers in enclosed spaces.

biophilia_count.description: Number of plant entities placed in the bathroom.
biophilia_count.rationale: Even minimal greenery in bathrooms provides restorative cues. Kaplan & Kaplan (1989) identify plant presence as a reliable restoration trigger in small enclosed spaces.
